attrs.alt: Global Secure Access traffic forwarding profiles and classifier

content.text: What are Traffic Forwarding Profiles?

Traffic Forwarding Profiles are the mechanism by which the Global Secure Access client decides which traffic to tunnel through the Microsoft SSE edge and which to send directly to the internet. Each profile covers a distinct traffic class: Microsoft 365 endpoints, private application segments, and general internet traffic. Profiles can be enabled independently, allowing a phased rollout starting with M365 traffic before expanding to internet filtering.

The GSA client evaluates profiles in priority order. Private Access rules are checked first (most specific), then M365 rules, then Internet Access (catch-all). A bypass rule at any priority level causes matching traffic to skip the SSE edge entirely.
Three Forwarding Profiles
Each profile covers a distinct category of traffic and is independently enableable.
Profile
Traffic Covered
Key Destinations
Requires
Microsoft 365
M365 Optimise and Allow endpoints (per Microsoft 365 URL list)
*.office.com, *.microsoft.com, *.sharepoint.com, login.microsoftonline.com
Entra P1
Private Access
IP ranges and FQDNs defined in Quick Access / Enterprise App segments
On-prem servers, Azure VNet resources, internal web apps
Entra P1 + Private Access licence
Internet Access
All other internet-bound traffic not matched by the above profiles
Public web, SaaS apps, CDNs
Entra P1 + Internet Access licence
Enabling Profiles via Graph API
Profiles are enabled at the tenant level via Graph API or the Entra portal under Global Secure Access → Connect → Traffic forwarding. Start with one profile per rollout phase.

# Enable all three forwarding profiles via Graph API
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'NetworkAccess.ReadWrite.All'

# List current forwarding profiles and their state
$profiles = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/networkAccess/forwardingProfiles'
$profiles.value | Select-Object id, displayName, state, trafficForwardingType

# Enable Microsoft 365 profile
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH `
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/networkAccess/forwardingProfiles' `
  -Body (@{ trafficForwardingType = 'microsoft365'; state = 'enabled' } | ConvertTo-Json)

# Enable Private Access profile
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH `
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/networkAccess/forwardingProfiles' `
  -Body (@{ trafficForwardingType = 'private'; state = 'enabled' } | ConvertTo-Json)

# Enable Internet Access profile
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH `
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/networkAccess/forwardingProfiles' `
  -Body (@{ trafficForwardingType = 'internet'; state = 'enabled' } | ConvertTo-Json)
Microsoft 365 Profile: Auto-Managed Endpoint Rules

The M365 forwarding profile sources its IP and FQDN rules directly from the Microsoft 365 URL and IP address list published at endpoints.office.com. Microsoft automatically updates these rules when the M365 service changes endpoints — you do not maintain the list manually.

# List the IP/FQDN rules in the M365 forwarding profile
# These are auto-managed by Microsoft from the M365 URL and IP list
$profileId = (Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/networkAccess/forwardingProfiles' |
  Select-Object -ExpandProperty value |
  Where-Object { $_.trafficForwardingType -eq 'microsoft365' }).id

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/networkAccess/forwardingProfiles/$profileId/policies" |
  Select-Object -ExpandProperty value | ForEach-Object {
      Write-Host "Policy: $($_.displayName)"
      $_.policyRules | ForEach-Object { Write-Host "  Rule: $($_.destinations)" }
  }

# The M365 profile rule set is refreshed automatically by Microsoft as endpoints change
# You do not need to maintain the IP/FQDN list manually
Bypass Rules: Excluding Traffic from Tunnelling

Bypass rules tell the GSA client to send matching traffic directly to the destination without routing through the SSE edge. Use them for internal resources that do not need SSE inspection, latency-sensitive applications, or traffic already protected by other controls.
Group-Based Profile Assignment for Phased Rollout

By default, enabled profiles apply tenant-wide. Use targeted group assignment to roll out to a pilot group first, validate the experience, then expand. This is the recommended approach for all three profiles.

# Assign forwarding profiles to a specific user group (selective rollout)
# By default, forwarding profiles apply tenant-wide. Use group assignment for pilots.

$profileId = '<profile-id>'
$groupId   = '<pilot-group-id>'

# Check if group-based assignment is supported on this profile
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/networkAccess/forwardingProfiles/$profileId" |
  Select-Object id, displayName, state, assignmentType

# Set assignment type to 'targeted' and add the pilot group
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH `
  -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/networkAccess/forwardingProfiles/$profileId" `
  -Body (@{
      assignmentType = 'targeted'
      includedGroups = @( @{ id = $groupId } )
  } | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 4)
Phased Rollout Strategy
Enable profiles incrementally to reduce risk. Each phase adds more traffic to the SSE edge while the previous phase stabilises.
Phase
Profiles Enabled
Group Scope
Validation
Phase 1 — Pilot
M365 profile only
IT pilot group (50 users)
Verify O365 traffic appears in NetworkAccessTraffic
Phase 2 — Private apps
M365 + Private Access
IT + infrastructure teams
Confirm private app access works; no VPN needed
Phase 3 — Internet filtering
All three profiles
All employees in waves
Check block/allow rates; adjust security profile
Phase 4 — Full deployment
All three profiles
All managed devices
Decommission legacy proxy/VPN for covered traffic
KQL: Traffic Split by Profile
Monitor how much traffic is flowing through each profile to understand adoption and detect unexpected shifts in traffic patterns.

// Traffic volume split by forwarding profile type
NetworkAccessTraffic
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| summarize
    TotalGB  = round(sum(SentBytes + ReceivedBytes) / 1e9, 2),
    Sessions = count(),
    Users    = dcount(UserPrincipalName)
    by TrafficType
| order by TotalGB desc

// Daily trend per profile — detect sudden spikes
NetworkAccessTraffic
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| summarize GB = round(sum(SentBytes + ReceivedBytes) / 1e9, 2)
    by TrafficType, bin(TimeGenerated, 1d)
| order by TimeGenerated asc
KQL: Validating Bypass Rules

Confirm that bypass destinations are not appearing in tunnel logs. Traffic from bypassed destinations should be absent from NetworkAccessTraffic — if it appears, the bypass rule is misconfigured or not yet applied to the client.

// Verify bypass rules are working — these destinations should NOT appear in the logs
// Any matching traffic means bypass is NOT in effect and traffic is being tunnelled
NetworkAccessTraffic
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1d)
| where DestinationFQDN in ('internal-dns.corp.contoso.com') or
        ipv4_is_in_range(DestinationIP, '192.168.100.0/24')
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, DestinationFQDN, DestinationIP, TrafficType
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Profile Priority and Conflict Resolution
Scenario
Behaviour
Destination matches Private Access AND M365 profile
Private Access rule wins (higher priority)
Destination matches M365 profile AND Internet Access profile
M365 rule wins
Destination matches bypass rule in any profile
Traffic goes direct — bypass overrides tunnel rules
No profile matches the destination
Traffic goes direct (not tunnelled) if Internet Access profile is disabled
Internet Access profile enabled, destination unknown
Internet Access profile catches all remaining traffic
Diagnosing Profile Matching on the Client
When a user reports that traffic is not being tunnelled correctly, check the GSA client routing table and event log on the endpoint to confirm which profile matched the destination.

# Diagnose which forwarding profile is matching a given destination on the client
# Run on a Windows device with the GSA client installed

# Step 1: Check if the GSA tunnel adapter is active
Get-NetAdapter | Where-Object { $_.InterfaceDescription -like '*Global Secure Access*' } |
  Select-Object Name, Status, LinkSpeed

# Step 2: Check the routing table for GSA-managed routes
# GSA client injects routes into the routing table for tunnelled destinations
Get-NetRoute -InterfaceAlias 'Global Secure Access' | Select-Object DestinationPrefix, NextHop, RouteMetric

# Step 3: Verify DNS resolution is happening inside the tunnel (for private FQDNs)
Resolve-DnsName 'rdp.corp.contoso.com' -Server '168.63.129.16'

# Step 4: Review GSA client event log for profile policy application
Get-WinEvent -LogName 'Microsoft-Windows-GlobalSecureAccess/Operational' -MaxEvents 30 |
  Where-Object { $_.LevelDisplayName -in ('Error','Warning') } |
  Select-Object TimeCreated, LevelDisplayName, Message
Microsoft 365 Endpoint Categories

The M365 forwarding profile covers the Optimize and Allow categories from Microsoft's published endpoint list. The Default category — used by background telemetry and non-latency-sensitive endpoints — is excluded and falls through to the Internet Access profile or goes direct if Internet Access is disabled.

# Microsoft 365 endpoint categories included in the M365 forwarding profile
# The profile includes 'Optimize' and 'Allow' categories from endpoints.office.com
# 'Default' category traffic is NOT included — it goes via Internet Access or direct

# Verify current M365 endpoints via Microsoft API
$endpoints = Invoke-RestMethod `
  -Uri 'https://endpoints.office.com/endpoints/worldwide?clientrequestid=<guid>&NoIPv6=true'

# Count endpoints per category
$endpoints | Group-Object category | Select-Object Name, Count

# Optimize category: latency-sensitive (Teams media, Exchange, SharePoint)
$endpoints | Where-Object { $_.category -eq 'Optimize' } |
  Select-Object -ExpandProperty urls | Sort-Object | Get-Unique

# Note: Optimize endpoints are ~10 FQDNs; they carry ~75% of M365 traffic volume
# All these are included in the M365 forwarding profile automatically
KQL: Detecting Devices Not Using the GSA Tunnel for M365
Users who accessed Microsoft 365 apps during the day but do not appear in NetworkAccessTraffic may have the GSA client not installed, disabled, or bypassing the tunnel. Flag them for remediation.

// Identify devices with no GSA traffic in last 24h (may not have client installed)
// Requires a reference list of expected managed device owners
let active_users = NetworkAccessTraffic
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1d)
| summarize by UserPrincipalName;

// Cross-reference with sign-in logs to find users who authenticated but had no GSA traffic
SignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1d)
| where ResultType == 0
| where UserType == 'Member'
| where AppDisplayName has_any('Office 365','SharePoint','Exchange Online','Microsoft Teams')
| summarize by UserPrincipalName
| where UserPrincipalName !in (active_users)
| project UserPrincipalName, Status = 'Accessed M365 without GSA tunnel'
| order by UserPrincipalName asc
KQL: M365 Traffic Breakdown by App
Understand which Microsoft 365 workloads generate the most bandwidth through the M365 profile. This informs capacity planning and helps prioritise which bypass rules (if any) make sense.

// M365 traffic breakdown — which M365 apps generate the most volume
NetworkAccessTraffic
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where TrafficType == 'microsoft365'
| extend AppHint = case(
    DestinationFQDN has 'teams', 'Teams',
    DestinationFQDN has 'sharepoint', 'SharePoint',
    DestinationFQDN has 'outlook' or DestinationFQDN has 'exchange', 'Exchange',
    DestinationFQDN has 'graph.microsoft.com', 'Graph API',
    'Other M365')
| summarize GB = round(sum(SentBytes + ReceivedBytes) / 1e9, 3)
    by AppHint
| order by GB desc
Infrastructure as Code: Managing Profiles via Terraform
GSA forwarding profiles can be managed via the azapi Terraform provider or with null_resource local-exec blocks calling the Graph API. Native Terraform provider support was in preview as of 2024.
Summary
Global Secure Access uses three traffic forwarding profiles — Microsoft 365, Private Access, and Internet Access — to decide which traffic the GSA client tunnels through the SSE edge.
Profiles are independently enableable, supporting a phased rollout: start with M365, add Private Access, then enable Internet Access with web filtering.
The M365 profile auto-manages its IP/FQDN rules from Microsoft's published endpoint list — no manual maintenance required.
Bypass rules exclude specific FQDNs, IP subnets, or ports from tunnelling; they take priority over all tunnel rules.
Use targeted group assignment to pilot each profile with a small group before tenant-wide deployment.
The NetworkAccessTraffic Log Analytics table lets you monitor traffic volume, bypass rule effectiveness, and per-profile adoption over time.
